humanities_role_explanation: Uses real-world human-made art history exam questions and artwork images to diagnose domain-specific art-historical knowledge and visual reasoning capabilities in LLMs.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Combines natural language questions (English and Italian) with low-to-medium resolution artwork images to perform multimodal Visual Question Answering (VQA).
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates factual recognition of art history facts, visual reasoning over artwork composition/techniques, identification of incorrect words in scholarly/educational texts, and structured filling of text blanks.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: EduArt is specifically designed as a domain-specific benchmark for evaluating art history knowledge and visual reasoning.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates twelve major models including GPT-5.5, GPT-5.4 Mini, GPT-5.4 Nano, Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview, Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite Preview, Gemini 3.5 Flash, Claude Opus 4.6, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Claude Haiku 4.5, Qwen3-VL-235B, Mistral Large 3, and Pixtral Large.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Generative multimodal large language models are the primary systems being benchmarked.

arts_humanities_data.note: Employs datasets matching the Visual Arts and Performing Arts category, specifically focusing on Art History and Renaissance paintings.

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses exact match accuracy, F1 score, statement accuracy, element accuracy, blank-filling accuracy, and Classical Test Theory (CTT) item discrimination (point-biserial correlation).

english_language.note: The paper is written in English, and the benchmark evaluates both English (AP Art History) and Italian (Zanichelli) datasets.

summary: This paper introduces EduArt, an educational-level benchmark designed to evaluate art-historical knowledge and visual reasoning in multimodal LLMs using 871 human-authored questions. The questions are derived from Italian secondary-school materials and AP Art History exams, covering seven distinct formats including multiple choice, error identification, and word positioning. The study applies Classical Test Theory to assess item-level difficulty and discrimination, demonstrating that task format variation reveals significant performance drops in models that otherwise reach ceiling performance on multiple-choice questions.
key_findings: While six frontier models scored over 90% accuracy on standard multiple-choice questions, performance dramatically plummeted on more complex formats, such as error identification (dropping to 6.2% for Claude Sonnet 4.6) and open completion (dropping to 23.9% for Claude Opus 4.6). The study also noted that requiring models to justify their answers (motivation condition) had a largely negative, family-dependent effect, decreasing accuracy for GPT and Gemini models while slightly improving Claude models.
